title: Lesson 427
transcript: We just talked about how it's really important to involve security in the software acquisition process. So if you're an organization that doesn't develop your own software, you buy it instead. Security has to be involved right from the start and throughout. We just talked about that, but it doesn't really matter what kind of software it is, whether it's commercial off-the-shelf software or it's open source or third-party software that for example you've commissioned some software development company to produce for you or even if you're purchasing for example software as a service from a cloud provider It doesn't matter. Obviously we need to focus on the security requirements and ensure whatever it is that you're buying has the proper level of security built in. And that means security has to be at the table right at the start and throughout each of the phases of in this case software acquisition or purchase it's just like and we've said this many times in this course, right? If you're purchasing anything, it doesn't matter what it is, right? A service or a product, security has to be involved. And that's what we're focusing on in here, certainly because it's mentioned in the exam outline. If you're buying software, it doesn't matter what kind of software it is. Security has to be at the table to understand the requirements so that we can ultimately buy something that has the right level of security designed and built into it to ultimately protect the organization, its value, its reputation, its reputation. ability to comply to laws and regulations, including those that are focused on privacy, for example. The software has to be able to protect.
